segments.s767-g25: This is a noun phrase acting as the direct object of the verb that follows. فرستاده (messenger) is the head noun, followed by the determiner/quantifier دیگر (another) placed after the noun (a common Persian order). از انجمن is a prepositional phrase 'from the assembly' that specifies the messenger's origin and follows the noun.
segments.s767-g26: گزین کرد is a compound past tense verb (گزین + کرد) meaning 'chose' or 'selected'; کرد here functions as the auxiliary 'did/made' with the verb stem گزین. The words بینا‌دل و رای‌زن follow as an appositive phrase describing the chosen messenger: بینا‌دل is an adjective/epithet 'large‑hearted' and رای‌زن is a nominal role 'counselor', joined by و 'and' — together they form 'a large‑hearted counselor' applied to the messenger.
segments.s767-g17: بدست is a prepositional phrase 'by / by the hand of' placed before the agent. یکی 'a certain' is an indefinite determiner modifying the following name phrase. سعد وقاص نام places the proper name(s) before the postpositive نام ('named / called'), a poetic pattern meaning 'called Saʿd Waqāṣ'. Note the verb is omitted here (typical in poetry) and agency is expressed solely by بدست + name.
segments.s767-g18: This is a negative enumeration: نه introduces the negation and applies to the following nouns; و links elements (بوم و نژاد = 'homeland and lineage'), then another نه negates دانش and the final نه negates کام. The verb 'to have' (داشت) is omitted and understood, so the line states what he lacked.
segments.s767-g19: کنون 'now' begins the clause; تا here has the sense 'as far as / even as for' marking extent/position, followed by the locative در طیسفون 'in Ctesiphon'. لشکرست is an archaic fused form where لشکر 'army/host' is followed by the contracted copula -ست (from است), meaning 'there is a host'.
segments.s767-g20: همین 'this very' emphasizes the following noun. زاغ پیسه is a compound: زاغ 'crow/rook' used pejoratively and پیسه 'pittance' (here describing the crow as paid cheaply). به پیش is a locative 'before / in the presence of', and اندرست (from اندر + است) is an archaic present copula meaning 'is present / stands among'. The clause thus says this cheaply‑paid crow stands before them.
segments.s767-g21: تو is the vocative/subject 'you', followed by the instrumental phrase با لشکرت 'with your army'. رزم را uses the object marker را to mark 'the battle' as the thing to be arranged. ساز کن is an imperative: ساز (here 'arrangement / means') with کن 'do' — 'prepare/arrange'.
segments.s767-g22: سپه را 'the host' marked by را is the object. برین is the poetic grouping of بر + این ('upon/against this'); here ین is the orthographic variant of این. The next بر is a repeated preposition for emphasis, and هم‌آواز 'in unison / of one accord' is the complement; کن is the imperative 'make / do' — 'make the host be united upon this.'
segments.s767-g23: من 'I' is the subject; اینک پس are adverbial particles 'now, then' (پس giving a sense of 'so/then'). نامه‌بر سان is a simile: نامه‌بر 'letter‑bearer / envoy' + سان 'like/as'; باد is the optative particle (archaic for 'may it be' or a condensed form of باشد), so the whole clause is a wish: 'may I be like a letter‑bearer.'
segments.s767-g24: بیایم is in the subjunctive/optative sense 'may I come' (dependent wish with باد earlier). به نزد 'to the presence of' gives the goal, and تو‌ای is the vocative 'you, O ...' (poetic order with pronoun + ای). پاک و راد are epithets 'pure and generous' addressing the addressee.
segments.s767-g9: خداوند is the head noun 'the lord/sovereign'. گردنده is an agentive/participial adjective ('the one who turns' or 'turner') attached to خداوند; together they mean 'the lord who turns'. The direct objects بهرام و هور follow without an explicit object marker in this poetic line, so the whole phrase reads 'the lord who turns Bahram and Hūr.'
segments.s767-g10: This is a parallel, emphatic construction repeating خداوند: 'the lord of the elephant and the lord of the ant.' Each خداوند governs the following noun; the repetition stresses mastery over both large and small.
segments.s767-g11: کند is the main verb 'does/makes' (here with implicit subject carried from the previous line—God). چون بخواهد is a temporal/subordinate clause 'when/whenever He wishes' with بخواهد in the subjunctive/relative sense 'wills'. ز ناچیز چیز is a prepositional phrase 'from the insignificant (a) thing' — poetic word order places the source phrase after the verb.
segments.s767-g12: که introduces a relative clause modifying 'چیز' ('a thing which...'). آموزگارش 'his/its teacher' carries the possessive -ش referring back to that thing or agent in context. نباید here is negative but idiomatically indicates inability or that it is not for (i.e., not possible for) the teacher to do the same; به نیز ('to/for also') is a compact, poetic phrase meaning roughly 'also' or 'likewise'. The whole clause therefore means 'which even its teacher cannot do likewise.'
segments.s767-g13: بگفت is the reporting verb 'he said'. آنک introduces indirect speech ('that'/'for'), and ما را is the experiencer/object 'to us'. چه آمد is 'what came/happened', and بروی here is a poetic verbal/adpositional element meaning 'upon (us)' or 'befell' — together: 'he said that what had befallen us.'
segments.s767-g14: وزین should be read as an attributive/poetic adjective phrase modifying پادشاهی (here 'this/dignified kingship' or 'from this kingship' depending on scansion); بشد is the past/perfective of شدن 'it became/it came to pass'. رنگ و بوی 'color and scent' are the complements: 'the kingship gained color and fragrance' (i.e., regained its appearance/glory).
segments.s767-g15: ز introduces a temporal/source phrase 'from/since'. The sequence 'ز رستم کجا کشته شد روز جنگ' is a condensed relative clause: 'from the day of battle when/where Rostam was killed.' کجا functions here to mark the place/occasion ('where/when') of کشته شد ('was killed').
segments.s767-g16: ز تیمار 'from/because of care/grief' — تیمار here means solicitude or sorrow. بر ما 'for/on us' marks the affected party. جهان گشت تنگ is a standard verbal phrase: جهان 'the world' + گشت (became) + تنگ (narrow) — 'the world became narrow (for us)'.
segments.s767-g1: دبیر جهاندیده را پیش خواند is a past‑tense clause with the direct object marked by the postposition را after the noun phrase. دبیر جهاندیده is a nominal phrase (دبیر 'scribe/scholar' modified by the adjective/epithet جهاندیده 'worldly‑wise'), پیش here means 'in the presence of / before (someone)', and خواند is a third‑person singular past verb meaning 'called/summoned' (classical poetic past). The word order is typical Persian: Object (دبیر جهاندیده + را) then adverbial/locative (پیش) then verb (خواند).
segments.s767-g2: دل آگنده بودش همه برفشاند contains two clauses: دل آگنده بودش = 'his heart was full' where آگنده is a predicative adjective 'filled/overflowing' and بودش is the past copula بود with the enclitic ش 'his/it‑was‑his'. The second part همه برفشاند is a simple past clause with an omitted subject; همه 'all' functions as the object/quantifier and برفشاند is the past tense verb 'scattered/dispensed'. The sequence expresses a state (heart full) followed by an action (he scattered it all); inversion of elements is poetic but grammatical.
segments.s767-g3: جهاندار چون کرد آهنگ مرو: چون + کرد forms a temporal conjunctional phrase 'when/as he made', with جهاندار 'the sovereign' as subject. آهنگ is a noun meaning 'intention/aim' and takes the following place name (مرو, here Merv) as its object/goal: 'made the intention to go to Merv'. Note that in classical Persian آهنگ + place expresses the purpose of the action; کرد is the past verb 'did/made'.
segments.s767-g4: به ماهوی سوری کنارنگ مرو is an admonitory/imperative clause. به is the preposition 'to/towards', introducing the place phrase ماهوی سوری کنارنگ (a sequence of epithets or place‑names). The final مرو here is the negative imperative form 'do not go' (in classical/poetic usage identical in form to the place name Merv), so the clause reads as a prohibition 'do not go to ...'. Word order places the destination before the imperative verb for emphasis, a poetic inversion.
segments.s767-g5: یکی نامه بنوشت با درد و خشم is a past narrative clause: یکی is an indefinite pronoun 'someone / a certain one' introducing the agent, نامه 'letter' is the direct object, and بنوشت is the poetic/prefixed past form 'wrote' (بـ‑ + نوشت). The prepositional phrase با درد و خشم 'with pain and anger' describes the writer's emotional state; با marks manner or accompaniment.
segments.s767-g6: پر از آرزو دل پر از آب چشم is two parallel nominal predicates: پر از آرزو 'full of longing/desire' and دل پر از آب چشم 'the heart full of the water of the eye (i.e. full of tears)'. پر is used predicatively as 'full (of)' with the preposition از introducing the content. The line uses poetic parallelism: the first phrase qualifies the (implicit) speaker's state of mind, the second specifies the heart filled with tears.
segments.s767-g7: نخست آفرین کرد بر کردگار: نخست is an adverb 'first' or 'initially'. آفرین here functions as a noun 'praise' or an acclamation and together with کرد ('made/did') forms the verbal expression 'praised' (literally 'made praise'). بر is the preposition 'upon/toward' and کردگار is the object 'the Creator/God' — so: 'First he offered praise to the Creator.'
segments.s767-g8: خداوند دانا و پروردگار is an appositive/epithet phrase naming and describing the divine addressee: خداوند 'God/Lord' followed by epithets دانا 'wise' and پروردگار 'nurturer/Lord'. The coordinating و links the two epithets; the whole phrase completes/renames کردگار from the previous segment.

sentences.s767-s13: He chose another messenger from the assembly — a large‑hearted counselor.
sentences.s767-s9: He was killed by a certain man called Saʿd Waqāṣ — he had neither homeland nor lineage, nor learning, nor desire.
sentences.s767-s10: Now, as far as Ctesiphon there is a host; and this very crow, paid a pittance, stands before (us).
sentences.s767-s11: You, with your army, prepare for battle; make the host be of one accord on this.
sentences.s767-s12: May I now, then, be like a letter‑bearer; may I come to you, O pure and generous one.
sentences.s767-s5: The sovereign who turns Bahram and Hūr — the lord of the elephant and the lord of the ant.
sentences.s767-s6: He makes, whenever He wishes, out of the insignificant a thing which even its teacher cannot (bring about).
sentences.s767-s7: He said: 'What has come upon us? From this kingship the color and scent appeared.'
sentences.s767-s8: From the day Rostam was slain in battle, because of our grief/care the world grew narrow for us.
sentences.s767-s1: He summoned the worldly‑wise scribe. His heart was full, and he scattered all.
sentences.s767-s2: When the sovereign made ready to go to Merv — (they cried) 'Do not go to Mahuy Suri, Kenar‑ang; do not go.'
sentences.s767-s3: Someone wrote a letter, full of pain and anger; (it said) 'My heart is full of longing, my heart full of tears.'
sentences.s767-s4: First he praised the Creator — God, the Wise and the Lord.
